definition: The replacement cost of property minus depreciation. This is often the basis for settling property damage claims, meaning you receive what the item was worth at the time of loss, not the cost of buying a new replacement.
examples: A 5-year-old laptop originally worth $1,500 might have an ACV of $400 after depreciation.

definition: A person or organization added to an insurance policy who is afforded coverage under the policy in addition to the named insured. This is common in commercial liability policies.
examples: A landlord may require a tenant to add them as an additional insured on the tenant's liability policy.

definition: A person who investigates insurance claims and recommends settlement amounts to the insurance company. Adjusters may be employed by the insurer (staff adjuster), work independently (independent adjuster), or represent the policyholder (public adjuster).
The maximum total amount an insurer will pay for all covered losses during a policy period, typically one year. Once the aggregate is reached, no further claims will be paid.
examples: A policy with a $1M per-occurrence limit and $2M aggregate will pay no more than $2M total in a year.

definition: A method of dispute resolution where an impartial third party (arbitrator) makes a binding decision. Many insurance policies include mandatory arbitration clauses for coverage disputes.
The transfer of rights or interest in an insurance policy from one party to another. Most policies require the insurer's consent before an assignment is valid.
A temporary agreement providing insurance coverage until a formal policy is issued. Binders are typically valid for 30-90 days and contain the essential terms of coverage.
Physical harm to a person, including sickness, disease, or death. Bodily injury liability coverage pays for injuries you cause to others.
examples: If you cause a car accident and the other driver is injured, your bodily injury liability coverage would apply.

definition: The termination of an insurance policy before its expiration date, either by the insured or the insurer. Cancellation by the insurer typically requires advance notice (often 30 days) and must comply with state regulations.
A document issued by an insurer or agent verifying that insurance coverage exists. It summarizes key policy information but does not confer any rights.
A formal request by the policyholder to the insurance company for payment or coverage under the terms of the policy after a covered loss occurs.

A provision requiring the policyholder to insure property to a stated percentage of its value (typically 80%). Failure to meet this requirement results in a penalty reducing the claim payment proportionally.
examples: If a building worth $500K is insured for only $300K under an 80% coinsurance clause, a $100K loss would only be paid at 75% ($75K).

definition: Auto insurance coverage that pays for damage to your vehicle resulting from a collision with another vehicle or object, regardless of fault.
Auto insurance coverage that pays for damage to your vehicle from non-collision events such as theft, vandalism, hail, fire, flooding, or animal strikes.
The section of an insurance policy that outlines the duties, rights, and obligations of both the insurer and the insured. Failure to meet conditions can void coverage.
In contract law, something of value exchanged between parties. In insurance, the consideration from the insured is the premium payment; from the insurer, it is the promise to pay covered losses.
The scope of protection provided under an insurance policy, including the types of losses, perils, and amounts the insurer will pay for.
The first page(s) of an insurance policy containing essential information: named insured, policy number, coverage amounts, deductibles, effective dates, and premiums. Often called the 'dec page.'
The amount the policyholder must pay out of pocket before insurance coverage begins to pay for a covered loss. Higher deductibles generally result in lower premiums.
examples: With a $500 deductible, if you have a $2,000 claim, the insurer pays $1,500 and you pay $500.

definition: The decrease in value of property over time due to wear, age, and obsolescence. Used to calculate actual cash value in property claims.
The date on which insurance coverage begins under a policy. Claims for losses occurring before this date are not covered.
A written amendment to an insurance policy that modifies coverage by adding, deleting, or changing provisions. Also called a rider.
examples: A scheduled personal property endorsement adds coverage for specific high-value items like jewelry.

definition: A provision in an insurance policy that eliminates coverage for specific risks, perils, or circumstances. Exclusions are found in the policy's exclusions section.
examples: Most homeowner's policies exclude flood damage, requiring a separate flood policy.

definition: A contract clause that frees parties from obligation when extraordinary events beyond their control occur, such as natural disasters, wars, or pandemics.
A specified time after a premium due date during which the policy remains in force even though the premium has not been paid. Typically 30 days for most policies.
A contractual agreement in which one party agrees to not hold the other party liable for damages, losses, or claims. Common in lease agreements and service contracts.
examples: A lease may include a hold harmless clause where the tenant agrees not to sue the landlord for certain types of damage.

definition: A contractual obligation of one party to compensate another for losses or damages. The principle of indemnity in insurance means restoring the insured to their pre-loss financial position.
A financial stake in the subject of insurance such that the insured would suffer a financial loss if the insured event occurred. Required for a valid insurance contract.
The termination of an insurance policy due to non-payment of premiums or failure to renew. A lapsed policy provides no coverage.
Legal responsibility for one's actions or failure to act. Liability insurance protects the insured against claims resulting from injuries or damage to other people or property.
A legal claim on property as security for a debt. In insurance, a lienholder (such as a mortgage company) is listed on the policy and must be paid before the property owner.
A person or organization listed on an insurance policy to receive payment in the event of a loss. Commonly used when a lender holds an interest in insured property.

A voluntary dispute resolution process where a neutral third party helps the disputing parties reach a mutually acceptable agreement. Unlike arbitration, the mediator does not make a binding decision.
The person or entity specifically identified as the insured in the declarations page of a policy. The named insured has full rights under the policy.
Failure to exercise the degree of care that a reasonably prudent person would exercise under similar circumstances. Negligence is a common basis for liability claims.

A legally binding contract establishing a confidential relationship between parties. The party receiving confidential information agrees not to disclose it to third parties. Also called NDA or confidentiality agreement.
A contractual agreement restricting one party from engaging in competing business activities for a specified time and geographic area after the relationship ends. Common in employment contracts.
An event or series of related events that results in a loss. Occurrence-based policies cover losses that occur during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is filed.
A specific cause of loss, such as fire, theft, windstorm, or vandalism. Policies may cover all perils except those excluded (open peril) or only specifically listed perils (named peril).
In insurance, harm other than bodily injury, including false arrest, detention, imprisonment, malicious prosecution, defamation, slander, libel, invasion of privacy, and wrongful eviction.
The maximum amount an insurer will pay for a single covered loss or during a policy period. Once the limit is reached, the insured is responsible for any additional costs.
The amount of money the insured pays to the insurance company for coverage. Premiums may be paid monthly, quarterly, semi-annually, or annually.
Proportional distribution or allocation. In insurance, pro rata cancellation means the insurer returns the unearned premium proportional to the remaining policy term.
The continuation of an insurance policy for an additional period after the current term expires. Renewal terms may differ from the original policy.
The amount needed to replace damaged or destroyed property with property of similar kind and quality at current prices, without deduction for depreciation.
examples: If your 10-year-old roof is destroyed, replacement cost coverage pays for a brand new roof of similar quality.

definition: An amendment or addition to an insurance policy that modifies its terms or scope of coverage. Also called an endorsement.
A sum of money paid by a tenant to a landlord as security against damage to the rental property or unpaid rent. Laws governing security deposits vary by jurisdiction.
A contract clause stating that if one provision is found invalid or unenforceable, the remaining provisions continue in full force. Also called a savings clause.
The right of an insurer, after paying a loss, to pursue the third party responsible for causing the loss to recover the amount paid. The insured must cooperate with subrogation efforts.
examples: After paying your claim for a car accident caused by another driver, your insurer can seek reimbursement from the at-fault driver's insurer.

definition: The period of time during which an insurance policy provides coverage, typically stated as an effective date and expiration date.
A wrongful act, other than a breach of contract, that results in injury to another person or damage to their property. Tort law is the basis for most liability insurance claims.
A liability policy that provides additional coverage above the limits of underlying policies such as auto or homeowner's. It kicks in when underlying policy limits are exhausted.
Coverage that protects you when the at-fault driver's liability limits are insufficient to cover your damages. It pays the difference between their limits and your actual damages.
The process by which an insurer evaluates the risk of insuring a particular person or property and determines the terms and premium for coverage.
Coverage that protects you if you are injured by a driver who has no liability insurance. It covers bodily injury and, in some states, property damage.
The voluntary relinquishment of a known right. In insurance, a waiver of subrogation prevents the insurer from seeking recovery from a third party.
Dismissal of an employee in violation of legal protections, contractual terms, or public policy. May give rise to claims for damages including lost wages and emotional distress.
